elements.sent-2411-001: While deep learning models are powerful tools that revolutionized many areas, they are also vulnerable to noise as they rely heavily on learning patterns and features from the exact details of the clean data.
elements.sent-2411-002: Transformers, which have become the backbone of modern vision models, are no exception.
elements.sent-2411-003: Current Discrete Wavelet Transforms (DWT) based methods do not benefit from masked autoencoder (MAE) pre-training since the inverse DWT (iDWT) introduced in these approaches is computationally inefficient and lacks compatibility with video inputs in transformer architectures.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we present RobustFormer, a method that overcomes these limitations by enabling noise-robust pre-training for both images and videos; improving the efficiency of DWT-based methods by removing the need for computationally iDWT steps and simplifying the attention mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-005: To our knowledge, the proposed method is the first DWT-based method compatible with video inputs and masked pre-training.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our experiments show that MAE-based pre-training allows us to bypass the iDWT step, greatly reducing computation.
elements.sent-2411-007: Through extensive tests on benchmark datasets, RobustFormer achieves state-of-the-art results for both image and video tasks.
elements.sent-2411-008: Addressing robustness in deep learning models for video data is crucial for practical applications, including surveillance, autonomous driving, and multimedia content analysis \cite{liu2022towards .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Just as images can suffer from distortions such as blurring, noise, and artifacts, video data experiences similar issues, compounded by the additional variability introduced over time \cite{pan2024svastin .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Deep learning models, especially those trained on clean, high-quality data, are highly sensitive to these disruptions because they rely heavily on precise patterns within the data to make accurate predictions \cite{tong2022videomae .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Even minor noise or inconsistencies in a single frame can cause the model to misinterpret important features, as these models have not been trained to ignore unexpected distortions \cite{schiappa2023large .}
elements.sent-2411-012: With video data, the challenge becomes even greater due to the temporal nature of the information, where each frame builds on previous ones to create a coherent sequence \cite{wu2024waveformer .}
elements.sent-2411-013: For instance, temporal inconsistencies such as random noise across frames, motion blur, flickering shadows, and heat-induced turbulence can lead to abrupt changes between frames, confusing the model as it attempts to track and interpret motion or recognize actions \cite{feichtenhofer2022masked,wu2024waveformer .}
elements.sent-2411-014: In tasks like autonomous driving, these inconsistencies are highly prevalent and pose significant risks, as the model may fail to accurately detect objects, assess distances, or predict movement patterns \cite{razi2023deep .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{experiment_results/teaser_ucf.png} \caption{Accuracy versus relative robustness of action recognition models on UCF-101} \label{fig:teaser}
elements.sent-2411-015: A primary reason for these issues lies in the pixel-based design of most deep-learning models.
elements.sent-2411-016: Although RGB pixel representations are the standard in vision applications because they capture detailed color and spatial information, they are computationally demanding and highly sensitive to noise and domain shifts.
elements.sent-2411-017: This sensitivity arises since RGB data represents exact pixel-level details, so even slight distortions can disrupt deep-learning models that depend on these patterns for accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-018: Additionally, RGB representations process both essential and irrelevant features equally, limiting their efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-019: Inspired by image compression techniques like JPEG, frequency domain transformations such as Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) provide a solution by isolating low-frequency components (representing significant information) from high-frequency ones (less relevant details) \cite{li2023discrete, park2023rgb .}
elements.sent-2411-020: This separation reduces memory demands and increases robustness to noise without compromising performance \cite{eskicioglu1995image, gao2021neural, li2023discrete .}
elements.sent-2411-021: While pure frequency domain transformations offer these benefits, they also lead to a loss of spatial or temporal locality due to the change in basis, a consequence of the Heisenberg uncertainty principle.
elements.sent-2411-022: In contrast, Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) provides a more balanced approach, decomposing the signal into both high- and low-frequency components across multiple scales.
elements.sent-2411-023: This multi-resolution representation allows DWT to retain spatial and temporal information, preserving important features and contextual details while still isolating high-frequency information.
elements.sent-2411-024: Image-based methods leveraging DWT have shown promise in enhancing robustness against spatial noise by allowing models to focus on frequency-based features that are less affected by disruptions \cite{Li_2020_CVPR .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Applying these principles to video data can address both temporal and spatial corruptions, as DWT can help models focus on low-frequency information, improving robustness to noise and other distortions across frames.
elements.sent-2411-026: In this paper, we employ a Video Masked Autoencoding approach \cite{NEURIPS2022_416f9cb3, Wang_2023_CVPR , additionally, incorporating DWT for pre-training and fine-tuning on the uncorrupted datasets, followed by evaluation on their corrupted counterparts.}
elements.sent-2411-027: We use UCF101 and HMDB51 datasets for training and evaluation, where the generated corruptions are of 6 types(including 5 spatial and 1 temporal corruptions in 5 severity levels) reflecting real world scenarios \cite{yi2021benchmarking .}
elements.sent-2411-028: In summary, our contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-029: We present a novel Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) based masked autoencoder architecture that is robust to spatial and temporal corruptions in both video and image data.
elements.sent-2411-030: To our knowledge this is the first work to implement DWT in Masked autoencoder setting.
elements.sent-2411-031: We perform comprehensive evaluation on several real-world noise types with varying severity levels in large-scale benchmark datasets.
elements.sent-2411-032: We demonstrate that our method performs equally well and in many cases better than commonly used IDWT variant which requires comparatively more compute resource and makes architectures overly complicated.
elements.sent-2411-033: BERT introduced the concept of Masked Language Modeling (MLM), a novel pretraining objective for natural language understanding, which led to significant advances in NLP \cite{kenton2019bert .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Following BERT's success, various NLP models, including RoBERTa \cite{Liu2019RoBERTaAR , ALBERT \cite{Lan2019ALBERTAL , and ELECTRA \cite{clark2020electra , extended this masked training approach.}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Inspired by BERT's masked token prediction, the concept of Masked Image Modeling (MIM) emerged for computer vision tasks.
elements.sent-2411-036: BEiT \cite{bao2022beitbertpretrainingimage extended this concept by proposing a discrete variational autoencoder (dVAE) to tokenize image patches, predicting masked tokens in a BERT-style pretraining.}
elements.sent-2411-037: These approaches demonstrated that MIM could effectively learn transferable representations for various downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-038: Masked Autoencoders (MAE) \cite{He_2022_CVPR refined MIM by reconstructing masked regions in images, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks using Vision Transformers (ViTs) and large-scale unlabeled data.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Building on masked training in language and image domains, Masked Video Modeling (MVM) has emerged to capture both temporal and spatial representations from video data.
elements.sent-2411-040: VideoMAE \cite{NEURIPS2022_416f9cb3 extends MAE to videos by applying random masking across both spatial and temporal dimensions, enabling the model to learn robust spatio-temporal representations, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:compare (a).}}
elements.sent-2411-041: VideoMAE achieves state-of-the-art performance on numerous video classification tasks, highlighting the effectiveness of masked training for video data.
elements.sent-2411-042: Similarly, BEVT (BEiT for Video) \cite{Wang_2022_CVPR extends BEiT's tokenization and masked prediction strategy to video patches, achieving strong results in action recognition tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Wavelets are essential in time-frequency analysis and signal processing tasks, such as anti-aliasing and detail restoration, through Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) and its inverse (IDWT) \cite{mallat1989theory .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Early studies combined wavelets with shallow neural networks for function approximation and classification, optimizing wavelet parameters within the network \cite{szu1992neural .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Recently, deeper networks have adopted wavelets for image classification, although these integrations can be computationally intensive \cite{de2020multi .}
elements.sent-2411-046: The Multi-level Wavelet CNN (MWCNN) \cite{Liu_2018_CVPR_Workshops applies Wavelet Packet Transform (WPT) for image restoration, handling both low- and high-frequency components.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Similarly, the Convolutional-Wavelet Neural Network (CWNN) \cite{DUAN2017255 utilizes dual-tree complex wavelet transform (DT-CWT) to reduce noise in SAR images while preserving crucial features, though within a simplified two-layer structure.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Wavelet Pooling \cite{williams2018wavelet employs a two-level DWT for pooling and combines DWT/IDWT for backpropagation, deviating from conventional gradient methods.}
elements.sent-2411-049: A key advantage of wavelets is their ability to provide sparse representations, which enables more efficient processing and faster model training \cite{williams2018wavelet,li2020wavelet,wu2024waveformer .}
elements.sent-2411-050: DWT-based training reduces redundancy, making it well-suited for real-time video classification tasks where small shifts in data can significantly affect model output.
elements.sent-2411-051: Unlike other approaches, our method avoids reconstruction after the wavelet transform, thus saving considerable computational resources, especially when handling high-resolution video data.
elements.sent-2411-052: This streamlined process enhances efficiency while maintaining robust performance in noisy environments.
elements.sent-2411-053: Robustness in video classification has been an ongoing research focus, initially centered on adapting image robustness techniques to video data.
elements.sent-2411-054: For instance, \cite{hendrycks2018benchmarking introduced benchmarks for evaluating image classifiers’ robustness against corruptions, a framework later adapted to videos by \cite{hirata2021making to assess model performance under various perturbations.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Addressing the challenge of temporal consistency in video data, models such as the two-stream network \cite{NIPS2014_00ec53c4 and 3D convolutions \cite{8099985 were developed, with data augmentation methods like temporal jittering and frame dropping \cite{Shorten2019ASO further enhancing robustness.}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Since DWT has been widely applied in noisy data reconstruction, achieving notable gains in both noise removal and complex tasks like deblurring \cite{williams2018wavelet,li2020wavelet , our approach builds on the methodologies of WaveFormer \cite{Wu_Sun_Xuan_Liu_Yan_2024 and WaveCNets \cite{Li_2020_CVPR by incorporating DWT.}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: As illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:compare , (b) shows WaveFormer’s framework, which computes attention using only the low-frequency components of the query and key matrices.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Unlike WaveFormer, however, we omit DWT and inverse DWT (IDWT) for the value matrix, passing it directly.
elements.sent-2411-059: Similar to WaveCNets, we discard high-frequency components during the initial downsampling layer but extend this technique to 3D-DWT to handle temporal decomposition.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figure/compare_2.png} \caption{Comparison between different architectures designed for video tasks. (a) is the regular masked autoencoder \cite{tong2022videomae}, (b) is the DWT-based architecture with iDWT module \cite{wu2024waveformer}, and (c) is our proposed method.} \label{fig:compare}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{architecture/frame_2.png} \caption{The framework of RobustFormer. Our approach integrates spatio-temporal tube masking as well as multi-resolution feature transformation using DWT to handle real-world noise types.} \label{fig:frame}
elements.sent-2411-060: Compared to the classical VideoMAE \cite{tong2022videomae , our proposed method enhances robustness by employing 3D-DWT for spatio-temporal analysis; in contrast to WaveFormer \cite{wu2024waveformer , our approach eliminates the computationally expensive iDWT step, significantly improving efficiency, as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:compare .}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Our implementation incorporates a two-step wavelet transform.
elements.sent-2411-062: First, we utilize DWT’s efficient downsampling capability to generate embeddings for video patches.
elements.sent-2411-063: By leveraging 3D-DWT, we can process video data more effectively than stacking coefficients from 2D-DWT.
elements.sent-2411-064: This approach enables simultaneous handling of both temporal and spatial noise, ensuring robust feature extraction.
elements.sent-2411-065: The forward and backward propagation processes for this step are detailed below.
elements.sent-2411-066: Let $\mathbf{X ^{t\times h\times w\times c}$ be the original downsampled and clipped video sequence of sequence length $t$, height $h$, width $w$ and channels $c$.}
elements.sent-2411-067: We start with the generation of transformation matrices based on the selected wavelet filters.
elements.sent-2411-068: This step is critical as it defines the low-pass and high-pass filters that are applied in the transformation process.
elements.sent-2411-069: Wavelet Filter Coefficients: Define the wavelet by its low-pass (L) and high-pass (H) filter coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-070: For simplicity, only the depth dimension's matrix construction is shown here.
elements.sent-2411-071: We can replicate this for the height and width dimensions: \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_k=\text{ConstructMatrix}(\mathbf{L},k) \mathcal{H}_k=\text{ConstructMatrix}(\mathbf{H},k) \end{aligned}\end{equation} where, k $\in$ \{H, W, D\} represent each of the individual dimension.
elements.sent-2411-072: The forward pass thus involves applying these matrices to decompose the input data into its respective frequency sub-bands.
elements.sent-2411-073: The input tensor $\mathbf{X}$ is thus decomposed into eight sub-bands using the transformation matrices for depth $(\mathcal{L _D,\mathcal{H _D)$, height $(\mathcal{L _H,\mathcal{H _H)$, and width $(\mathcal{L _W,\mathcal{H _W)$, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:frame . \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathbf{X}_{LLL}=\mathcal{L}_D^T\mathcal{L}_H^T\mathcal{L}_W^T\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{X}_{LLH}=\mathcal{L}_D^T\mathcal{L}_H^T\mathcal{H}_W^T\mathbf{X}, \ldots, \mathbf{X}_{HHH}=\mathcal{H}_D^T\mathcal{H}_H^T\mathcal{H}_W^T\mathbf{X} \end{aligned} \label{eq:dwt3d}\end{equation} where, the matrix construction leads to generating matrix $\mathcal{M _k \in \{\mathcal{L _k, \mathcal{H _k\}$ for dimension corresponding to $k$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: The resulting matrix $\mathcal{M _k$ will have dimensions $\left(\left\lceil\frac k2\right\rceil,k\right)$ ,where $\left\lceil\cdot\right\rceil$ is the ceiling function that handles cases where $k$ is odd.}
elements.sent-2411-075: The ConstructMatrix function works as follows: \begin{equation} \mathcal{M}^{ij}_k=\begin{cases}f_{t+1} \mathrm{if} j=2i+t \mathrm{,} t<n 0 \mathrm{otherwise}\end{cases} \label{eq:dwt1d}\end{equation} where, where $t$ indexes the filter coefficients $[f_1,f_2,\ldots,f_n]$.
elements.lstlisting-2411-001: [language=Python] # INPUT: Image/ Video def self_attention(Q, K, V): # DWT on Q, K ; attention with Q_l,K_l (Q_l, Q_h),(K_l, K_h) = DWT1D(Q), DWT1D(K) # Return attention weighted Values return softmax((Q_l @ K_l.T)/sqrt(d_k)) @ V def PatchEmbedDWT(x): # perform: DWT, ops(avg.,omit,concat), conv dwt = [DWT_2D(x[i]) for i in range(dims)] conv_tensor = conv(ops(dwt)) # images/videos -> tube/patch masking embeddings = perform_masking(conv_tensor) return embeddings if __name__ == "__main__": encoder = Encoder(self_attention, ...) decoder = Decoder(self_attention, ...) ... # input: x embeddings = PatchEmbedDWT(x) encoder_out = encoder(embeddings, ...) ... # During Pretraining reconstructed_video = decoder(encoder_out) loss = mse_loss(x, reconstructed_video) # During Finetuning preds = clf_head(encoder_out) loss = CrossEntropyLoss(preds, target) ... loss.backward()
